title: उद्धव गीता - प्रमुख श्लोक
devanagariText:
एतावानेव लोकेऽस्मिन् पुंसां निःश्रेयसोदयः।
तीव्रेण भक्तियोगेन मनो मय्यर्पितं स्थिरम्॥१॥

मार्तिका दारवी लौही लेप्या चित्रा च सैकती।
मनोमयी मणिमयी प्रतिमाष्टविधा स्मृता॥२॥

मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः।
एवम्भूतो मद्भक्तो मे सर्वतो मुक्तिमाप्नुयात्॥३॥

न रोधयति मां योगो न साङ्ख्यं धर्म एव च।
न स्वाध्यायस्तपस्त्यागो यथा भक्तिर्ममोर्जिता॥४॥

भक्त्याहमेकया ग्राह्यः श्रद्धयाऽऽत्मा प्रियः सताम्।
भक्तिः पुनाति मन्निष्ठा श्वपाकानपि सम्भवात्॥५॥

स्वर्गापवर्गयोः पुंसां रसायां भुवि सम्पदाम्।
सर्वासामपि सिद्धीनां मूलं तच्छ्रद्धया मतम्॥६॥

योगस्त्रिविधो मया पूर्वं प्रोक्तः कल्याणकारकः।
ज्ञानयोगः कर्मयोगो भक्तियोगश्च सत्तमः॥७॥

त्रिभिरेव गुणैरेतैर्गुणिनां सर्वदेहिनाम्।
गुणेषु गुणसौम्यत्वं मद्भक्तस्तूदधेर्यथा॥८॥

सर्वभूतेषु यः पश्येद्भगवद्भावमात्मनः।
भूतानि भगवत्यात्मन्येष भागवतोत्तमः॥९॥

मत्कर्मकृन्मत्परमो मद्भक्तः सङ्गवर्जितः।
निर्वैरः सर्वभूतेषु यः स मामेति पाण्डव॥१०॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भागवतान्तर्गत उद्धवगीता प्रमुख श्लोकाः सम्पूर्णाः ॥
transliteration:
Etavane-va Loke'smin Pumsam Nihshreyasodayah.
Tivrena Bhaktiyogena Mano Mayyarpitam Sthiram.||1||

Martika Daravi Lauhi Lepya Chitra Cha Saikati.
Manomaye Manimaye Pratimashtavidha Smrita.||2||

Mayyarpita-Manobuddhir Yo Madbhaktah Sa Me Priyah.
Evambhuto Madbhakto Me Sarvato Muktim Apnuyat.||3||

Na Rodhayati Mam Yogo Na Sankhyam Dharma Eva Cha.
Na Svadhyayas Tapas-Tyago Yatha Bhaktir Mamorjita.||4||

Bhaktyaham Ekaya Grahyah Shraddhaya''tma Priyah Satam.
Bhaktih Punati Mannishtha Shvapakan-Api Sambhavat.||5||

Svargapavarga-yoh Pumsam Rasayam Bhuvi Sampadam.
Sarvasam Api Siddhinam Mulam Tachcchrad-dhaya Matam.||6||

Yogas Trividho Maya Purvam Proktah Kalyanakarakah.
Jnanayogah Karmayogo Bhaktiyogashcha Sattamah.||7||

Tribhireva Gunair Etair Guninam Sarvadehinam.
Guneshu Gunasaumyatvam Madbhaktas Tudadheryatha.||8||

Sarvabhuteshu Yah Pashyed Bhagavadbhavam Atmanah.
Bhutani Bhagavatyatmanyesha Bhagavato-ttamah.||9||

Matkarmakrin Matparamo Madbhaktah Sangavarjitah.
Nirvairah Sarvabhuteshu Yah Sa Mameti Pandava.||10||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavatantargata Uddhava Gita Pramukh Shlokah Sampurnah ||
hindiMeaning:
इस लोक में मनुष्यों के निःश्रेयस (परम कल्याण) का उदय इतने से ही होता है — तीव्र भक्तियोग से मन मुझमें अर्पित और स्थिर करना।

मिट्टी, काष्ठ, लोहा, लेप, चित्र, रेत, मनोमय और मणिमय — इस प्रकार प्रतिमा आठ प्रकार की कही गई है।

जिसने मन-बुद्धि मुझमें अर्पित की, वह मेरा भक्त मुझे प्रिय है। ऐसा भक्त सर्वत्र मुक्ति प्राप्त करता है।

न योग, न सांख्य, न धर्म, न स्वाध्याय, न तप, न त्याग मुझे उतना बाँध सकता जितनी मेरी उत्कृष्ट भक्ति।

केवल एकनिष्ठ भक्ति और श्रद्धा से मैं ग्राह्य हूँ, सत्पुरुषों को प्रिय आत्मा। भक्ति मन्निष्ठा चाण्डाल को भी जन्म से पवित्र करती है।

स्वर्ग, अपवर्ग (मोक्ष), पृथ्वी की सम्पदा, रसातल और समस्त सिद्धियों का मूल श्रद्धा माना गया है।

तीन प्रकार का कल्याणकारी योग मैंने पूर्व में बताया — ज्ञानयोग, कर्मयोग और भक्तियोग (जो सर्वोत्तम है)।

इन तीन गुणों में गुणसौम्यता जो — जैसे समुद्र में नदी मिलती है — मेरा भक्त ऐसा होता है।

जो सर्व भूतों में भगवद्भाव देखे और भूतों को भगवान् में देखे — वह भागवतोत्तम है।

मेरा कर्म करने वाला, मुझे परम मानने वाला, मेरा भक्त, संग-रहित, सर्व भूतों में निर्वैर — हे पाण्डव! वह मुझे प्राप्त करता है।
benefits: Contains Krishna's final and most intimate teachings to Uddhava
Reveals bhakti yoga as the supreme path surpassing jnana and karma
Teaches the highest standard of a Bhagavata (devotee)
Invokes deep philosophical understanding of devotion, detachment, and liberation
Considered the essence of Bhagavata Purana's teachings
Ideal for contemplation and philosophical study
description: Dedicated to Lord Krishna, the Uddhava Gita - Key Verses (उद्धव गीता - प्रमुख श्लोक) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Rooted in Shrimad Bhagavata Purana (Skandha 11), this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to reveal bhakti yoga as the supreme path surpassing jnana and karma as well as grant deep philosophical understanding of devotion, detachment, and liberation. Additionally, it is known to contain Krishna's final and most intimate teachings to Uddhava. This prayer is traditionally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Gita Jayanti, Janmashtami, and Ekadashi to invoke divine grace. At 10 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 7 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Uddhava Gita - Key Verses in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Uddhava Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: The Uddhava Gita (उद्धव गीता) — also called the Hamsa Gita — is the final and most intimate philosophical discourse delivered by Bhagavan Krishna to his beloved devotee, friend and minister Uddhava, just before Krishna's departure from this world at the end of the Yadava-vamsha. The teaching is recorded across chapters 6-29 of the eleventh skandha (book) of the Srimad Bhagavata Purana — twenty-four chapters in all, comprising over 1100 verses, making it the longest single discourse of Krishna in the entire Bhagavata corpus, longer even than the Bhagavad Gita. Where the Bhagavad Gita was spoken on the battlefield of Kurukshetra in the language of duty and yoga (kriya-yoga, jnana-yoga, bhakti-yoga), the Uddhava Gita is spoken in retirement at Prabhasa-kshetra in the more intimate, devotional, post-Vedic language of Bhagavata-dharma — the supreme path of bhakti as the king of yogas. (Source: Srimad Bhagavata Purana 11.6-29.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is the Uddhava Gita called Krishna's final and most intimate teaching?
seoFaqs.answer: The Uddhava Gita is delivered at the very end of Krishna's earthly leelas — after the Mausala curse has destroyed the Yadava clan at Prabhasa, after Balarama has departed in yogic samadhi, just before Krishna himself withdraws his form by the riverbank. Uddhava — Krishna's lifelong childhood friend, minister, cousin (son of Devabhaga, brother of Vasudeva), and the disciple closest to His heart — falls at His feet weeping, begging not to be left behind in this world without his Lord. Krishna responds with the entire 1100-verse Uddhava Gita: not because the world needs a new teaching (the Bhagavad Gita already exists), but because Krishna wishes to give His most beloved disciple a complete, condensed and final rahasyam (esoteric teaching) — distilling the essence of all His earlier teachings to Arjuna, and elevating bhakti above even jnana and karma as the supreme path. It is therefore called Krishna's testament — His parting gift to His most intimate devotee. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 11.6-7.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the central teaching of the Uddhava Gita on bhakti yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: The supreme teaching of the Uddhava Gita is the explicit declaration of bhakti as the superior path among all yogas — surpassing even jnana-yoga and karma-yoga. The famous verse Na rodhayati mam yogo na sankhyam dharma eva cha, na svadhyayas tapas tyago yatha bhaktir mamorjita (11.14.20) declares: Neither yoga (Patanjali's ashtanga), nor sankhya (philosophical analysis), nor dharma (righteous conduct), nor svadhyaya (Vedic study), nor tapa (austerity), nor tyaga (renunciation) — none of these binds Me as does My devotee's intense bhakti. The companion verse Bhaktyaham ekaya grahyah (11.14.21) confirms: I am attainable by bhakti alone — even chandalas (outcastes) are purified by it from birth itself. The Bhagavata's revolutionary doctrine is that bhakti is not a means to liberation but is itself the highest goal — surpassing moksha, since the devotee does not desire liberation but only loving service to the Lord. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 11.14.20-24.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the Uddhava Gita teach about the highest standard of a Bhagavata devotee?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 9 of these key verses (Bhagavata 11.2.45) gives the supreme definition of a Bhagavatottama — the highest devotee: Sarva-bhuteshu yah pashyed Bhagavad-bhavam atmanah, bhutani Bhagavaty atmany esha bhagavatottamah — He who sees the Bhagavad-bhava (the divine presence) of his own Atman in all beings, and sees all beings within the Bhagavan-Atman — he is the supreme Bhagavata. This verse establishes a graduated hierarchy of Bhagavata-bhaktas (11.2.45-47): the uttama (highest) sees the Lord in all and all in the Lord; the madhyama (intermediate) loves God, befriends His devotees, shows compassion to the innocent and ignores the hostile; the kanishtha (beginner) worships the deity in the temple but does not yet see the Lord in others. The teaching is practical and progressive: every devotee can identify his current stage and aspire upward. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 11.2.45-47.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the eight types of deity-image (pratima) worship taught in the Uddhava Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 2 of these key verses (Bhagavata 11.27.12) describes the eight valid types of pratima (image) for worshipping the Lord: Martika (clay/earth), Daravi (wood), Lauhi (metal — bronze, copper, brass, iron), Lepya (sculpted plaster or wax), Chitra (painted picture or two-dimensional image), Saikati (sand — temporary saikat-Linga made on riverbanks), Manomayi (mental image visualised in dhyana), and Manimayi (jewelled image — crystal, ruby, emerald). This eight-fold typology is the foundational Vaishnava-Bhagavata classification that legitimises every kind of devotional practice — from the elaborate jewelled deities of Tirupati and Srinathji, to the simple clay images of Janmashtami, to the painted pat-chitras of Puri, to the saikat-Linga made by pilgrims on the Narmada bank, to the purely mental Krishna of the meditator. The Lord declares: I am present equally in all eight forms when worshipped with bhakti. This verse is the doctrinal foundation for the universal Hindu acceptance of murti-puja in any form. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 11.27.12.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Uddhava Gita be recited or studied for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Uddhava Gita is most powerfully recited or studied on Gita Jayanti (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi — when Krishna spoke the Bhagavad Gita; the Uddhava Gita is its complement), Janmashtami (Krishna's birthday), all Ekadashi days (sacred to Vishnu), Vaikuntha Ekadashi (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi), Wednesday (Budhvar — Krishna's day) and Thursday (Guruvar — the day of the guru-tattva, since Uddhava is the disciple par excellence). The traditional path is to study one chapter daily for 24 days during Purushottama-Adhika Maas (the leap-month sacred to Vishnu), or to recite the key verses daily as part of the morning prayers. Vaishnava sannyasis of the Madhva, Ramanuja, Vallabha, Chaitanya and Nimbarka sampradayas all teach the Uddhava Gita as the apex Vaishnava philosophical text alongside the Bhagavata Purana itself. The text is especially recommended for renunciates, Vedanta students and householders contemplating retirement — since it was Krishna's parting teaching to the devotee about to be left behind in samsara. (Source: Vaishnava acharya tradition; Bhagavata sampradaya.)
